what_it_does: Defines a standardized, citation-driven research rubric and report template for evaluating self-hosted LLM chat-UI platforms as a front-end for a custom multi-agent fabric.
observations: Requires every factual claim to come from a live source fetched in the current session, with inline URLs.
Explicitly flags LICENSE and MCP support as the two highest-stakes claims that must be verified from primary sources.
The use-case is not a generic chat box: it requires server-initiated push of channel/member messages into custom UI panels.
Lists 10 standard evaluation dimensions covering architecture, extensibility, model support, auth, mobile, theming, fork/extend difficulty, license, maturity, and MCP support.
Ends with 4 priority scores (a-d) and a bottom-line paragraph, with explicit strength ratings.
Instructs researchers to return the full markdown as a message, not to write the report file themselves.
standouts: Strong anti-hallucination rule: 'Write NOTHING from memory' and mandatory live-source citations.
Explicit instruction to read the actual LICENSE file and note non-standard branding-protection or commercial clauses.
MCP support singled out as the fastest-moving feature and a primary-source verification target.
The 'discriminator' is server-initiated inbound-message surfacing into custom panels, not just OpenAI-compatible call-out.
External OpenAI-compatible call-out is explicitly called trivial and not a differentiator.
conventions: Markdown heading hierarchy with bold labels for dimensions and priorities.
Inline citation requirement embedded in the grounding rule.
Report output structure prescribed at the end.
concerns: The 'live source' requirement is strict but provides no mechanism to verify the researcher actually fetched live sources.
The rubric assumes a June 2026 present date, which may become stale quickly.
Scoring uses qualitative strength labels without a numeric scale or aggregation method.
No definition of 'recent reputable articles' or source authority thresholds.
notes: A strict, live-source, apples-to-apples evaluation rubric focused on whether an LLM chat UI can serve as a fabric front-end, especially for unsolicited server-initiated messages.
questions: Which three specific platforms are being evaluated?
Who validates that sources were fetched live during the session?
What is the precise definition of the 'fabric' beyond supervisor/channels/members?
How are the four priority strength ratings aggregated or compared across researchers?
Who is responsible for writing the final report file from the returned markdown?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the file states it is a shared rubric for platform evaluation and then provides exactly that structure and rules.
intention_signals: To force all researchers to produce comparable, verifiable reports that surface how well each platform can act as a fabric front-end, with special attention to legal terms and the hard problem of server-initiated UI updates.
novelty: The explicit 'discriminator' focus on server-initiated push of unsolicited channel/member messages into custom panels, plus the pairing of license branding-clause scrutiny with MCP verification as highest-stakes items.
contribution: Provides a common evaluation framework, mandatory verification points, and a prescribed report structure that a fused system would keep to ensure consistency across platform research reports.
summary_for_embedding: A shared research rubric and report template for evaluating self-hosted LLM chat-UI platforms as front-ends for a custom multi-agent fabric, emphasizing live-source verification, license and MCP verification, and server-initiated inbound-message surfacing.
intention_for_embedding: To standardize apples-to-apples platform evaluation reports focused on extensibility, external API call-out, mobile usability, code-extension cleanliness, and license/MCP verification.
